TextENGNew: "Maybe we should call it a day. You don't seem to be in the mood."
"Hm? ...Oh, that's not true at all. I'm sorry if I gave you that impression."

Takano-san had been peering through the viewfinder for a while now, but I hadn't seen her press the shutter once. She was keeping me company, but she clearly wasn't in any state to enjoy bird photography...
changeReason: Old says 'bird watching' while new correctly says 'bird photography' for 野鳥撮影 (bird photography/shooting), which changes the understanding of their activity.

TextENGNew: Ever since the new board's announcement of their policy changes, Takano-san had been visibly despondent.
...It was understandable. She was the one who had created the Irie Institute in the first place. She had built it so she could devote her life to researching Hinamizawa Syndrome.
To have all of that denied, to be told to wrap up her work within a few years... of course she was devastated.

And it wasn't as though she could spend the remaining time entirely on research. The budget was being cut in stages, and by the end, she would barely be able to handle the paperwork of shutting things down, let alone conduct any research.
...She was the granddaughter of the late Dr. Takano. The determination to complete her grandfather's unfinished work was what had driven her all this way.
Things had been going so smoothly until now that this sudden reversal must have hit her all the harder...

The upheaval among those pulling the strings was apparently severe. The board had been entirely replaced, and people and projects tied to the old Koizumi faction were being purged as a warning to others.
changeReason: Old says 'restructured' while new says 'purged'; the Japanese 見せしめ的な人事 means punitive personnel changes to make an example, and 'purged' conveys the vindictive intent more accurately than 'restructured.'

TextENGNew: Moreover, the wind's new direction showed no sign of changing. The chances of the Koizumi faction returning to power were slim, and no matter what appeals the Irie Institute made, expecting the same level of support was impossible.
"...Isn't there anything we can do?"
Takano-san murmured, as if to herself. It was sad, but... she was right. There was nothing anyone could do...
changeReason: Old omits the Japanese 独り言のように (as if talking to herself) and presents the narrator's agreement as a direct response; new captures the murmuring quality and reflective agreement more faithfully.

TextENGNew: "......At first, the board was talking about halting the research immediately. ...But after a great deal of persuasion, they agreed to a gradual wind-down over several years.
The people who helped us when the Irie Institute was first established are the ones who fought for that."
"...Heh. ...I see. So what appears to be the worst-case scenario is actually someone else's hard-won victory behind the scenes..."
"Three years was the best they could manage. I tried too, you know. I used the data you'd compiled to make the case to the board about how important this research is..."
"Only...... three more years, then."

Perhaps Takano-san didn't appreciate how hard I had worked to persuade the new board members. ...Her words carried the unmistakable tone of someone for whom the length of time was beside the point. What mattered was that her research was being taken away.
The realization that I had failed to meet her expectations, that I had let her down... as a man, it stung.
"The best I can do now is... work behind the scenes to secure enough funding so that you can research to your heart's content during whatever time remains. Sorry... I wish I could do more."
"It's all right. If that's the best you can do, Jirou-san... then I'm truly grateful for it."
...Her words cut a little. I wished she had softened them, but demanding that of someone so heartbroken would have been pathetic of me. At a time like this, I needed to understand her pain.
Besides, I knew how close she had been to the late Koizumi-sensei. She had even called him 'Grandpa.'
changeReason: Old translates おじいちゃん (grandpa) as 'Uncle' — different familial term, though overall context is preserved.

TextENGNew: For her, his death wasn't just a political upheaval or a shift in the wind. She had also lost someone who had always watched over her.
No matter how hard she tried to act tough and worldly... she was never meant for this world. ...She was just an ordinary woman.
changeReason: Old says 'act villainous' while new says 'act tough and worldly'; the Japanese 悪ぶって means putting on a tough/edgy front, not actually being villainous.

TextENGNew: But she had been captivated by Hinamizawa Syndrome, and continuing her research required power beyond her own. ...The hand that reached out to her came from the dark side of the world—a world she should never have entered.
Now she had been left all alone in that world, putting on a brave face.
Takano-san probably saw me as an unreliable man. ...And she was right—I wasn't the kind of person who could live up to her expectations. I didn't have that kind of power.
The least I could offer... was the sincere wish to remain her ally until the very end.

She was a woman of action, and that made it difficult to predict what she might do when backed into a corner. ...I wanted to believe she would never go that far, but... the worry that she might do something drastic lingered.
changeReason: Old reverses perspective: JPN says others cannot predict her actions (彼女の行動が読めない), but old says 'harder for her to determine what actions to take' — changes unpredictability to indecisiveness.

TextENGNew: If that moment ever came—that very last moment before she crossed the line—I hoped I could be her anchor. But I hadn't earned enough of her trust for that... and that inadequacy was shameful.
Of course, part of me wanted her to rely on me. As a man, naturally I did.
But that wasn't the point. ...She had been cast adrift in this world, utterly alone. Someone had to stand by her.
And if I was the only one who understood that, then wasn't it my responsibility?
I knew her well enough to know she would never ask for help. Even if she resented it as meddling, someone needed to stay close. I understood that.
I didn't think I was the right person for the job. ...Surely there were many people out there more reliable and better suited than me.
But that didn't mean it was acceptable to leave her alone until someone better came along...
"...Takano-san. I know I'm not much to rely on, and I might be a sorry excuse for a man, but..."
"Hm? What's this all of a sudden? What is it, Jirou-san?"
"Even so, if you'd lean on me, I think there are things I could help with. So..."
"Thank you. But I don't really believe that talking about things makes the burden lighter. I'm sorry. ...Though I truly am happy that you care."
"...I-I see. That's fine, then. ...Just know that I'll always be on your side. Please believe that."
"Of course I do. ...If you think of a way to convince the board, do let me know."
"Sure... I'll do what I can."
Why was it? I knew she was far stronger than I was, and that nothing someone like me could offer would make any difference.
And yet... I felt, somehow, that she needed help and support right now. She looked as composed as ever, but only I—who had spent so long looking at her through a lens—could tell the difference.
A viewfinder, after all, is also a window into someone's heart.
...But if I said something like that, she'd only tease me for being out of character. So I kept it to myself.
In any case... I had never felt more strongly that she needed someone beside her than I did right now...
